items.myth: A heap is a sorted list.
items.reality: Only the root is guaranteed to be the extremum. The array is partially ordered (parent ≤ children) but not fully sorted; you can't binary-search inside it.
items.example:
```python
import heapq
h = [1, 3, 2, 5, 4, 9, 8]
heapq.heapify(h)
h                # heap-ordered, NOT sorted: [1, 3, 2, 5, 4, 9, 8]
```

items.myth: Heaps are slow because they involve a tree.
items.reality: Heaps are stored as arrays — no pointers, excellent cache locality. push/pop are O(log n) and heapify is O(n).
items.example:
```python
import heapq
# Compact list — no nodes, no pointers:
heap = [5, 8, 3, 9, 4]
heapq.heapify(heap)   # in-place
```

items.myth: Heapify costs O(n log n) because each sift is O(log n).
items.reality: Bottom-up heapify is O(n). Most nodes are near the leaves where sift work is constant; only the few nodes near the root pay log n.
items.example:
```python
import heapq
big = list(range(1_000_000))
heapq.heapify(big)   # ~ linear time
```

items.reality: Both are min-priority structures, but `queue.PriorityQueue` adds thread-safety locking. In single-threaded or async code use `heapq` for less overhead.
items.example:
```python
from queue import PriorityQueue   # thread-safe; locks per op
import heapq                       # no locks, faster single-threaded
```
